suggestion: The topic is above threshold — no pivot needed. To strengthen novelty from 7 to 8+, sharpen the contribution around the penalty-induced non-stationarity: the claim that escalating penalties create qualitatively different equilibrium structure (phase transitions, symmetry breaking) compared to penalty-free timing games is the most publishable angle. Frame the paper as 'Dynamic Signal Detection Games with Accumulating Costs' rather than anchoring on the TV show — the Montage game is a motivating example, not the contribution. Two refinements: (1) Prove analytically that there exists a critical penalty threshold above which the symmetric BNE breaks — this is a stronger result than just observing it in RL. (2) Connect to the existing 'social SDT' literature (Sorkin & Dai 1994, Bahrami et al. 2010 on collective decision-making) to position the work at the intersection of computational game theory and cognitive science, which broadens the audience beyond pure ML venues to CogSci/AAMAS/NeurIPS.
